sentences.grammarNote: **こんにちは** = 'hello' (daytime greeting). The particle **は** after **私** makes 'I' the topic. **〜です** = is/am (polite copula). Japanese sentences typically end with the verb or copula.

sentences.base_language: I am a very famous music artist
sentences.target_language: 私はとても有名な音楽アーティストです
sentences.romanized: watashi wa totemo yuumei na ongaku aatisuto desu
sentences.literal: I [topic] very famous [adj] music artist am.
sentences.grammarNote: **有名な** (yūmei na) = famous; a **な**-adjective. The **な** appears when modifying a noun: **有名な人** (famous person). **とても** = very (intensifies adjectives). **音楽アーティスト** mixes a Japanese word with a katakana loanword.

sentences.base_language: I sang at the super bowl
sentences.target_language: 私はスーパーボウルで歌いました
sentences.romanized: watashi wa suupaa bouru de utaimashita
sentences.grammarNote: **で** marks the location where an action takes place: **スーパーボウルで歌う** = 'sing at the Super Bowl'. **歌いました** = sang (polite past): **歌う** (utau, to sing) → **歌います** → **歌いました**.

sentences.target_language: 私はプエルトリコ出身です
sentences.grammarNote: **出身** (shusshin) = origin/hometown: **出** (shutsu, exit/come out) + **身** (shin, body/self) — 'where one came from'. Pattern: [place] + **出身です** = 'I am from [place]'. No particle is needed between the place name and **出身**.

sentences.base_language: Do you want to learn one of my songs?
sentences.target_language: 私の曲を一つ覚えたいですか？
sentences.romanized: watashi no kyoku wo hitotsu oboetai desu ka?
sentences.grammarNote:
**曲** (kyoku) = song/musical piece: **曲** (kyoku, curve/tune) — on'yomi reading used in musical contexts.
**一つ** (hitotsu) = one (generic native counter). Placed after the object it counts: **曲を一つ** = 'one song'.
**覚えたいですか** = 'do you want to learn/memorize?': **覚える** (oboeru, memorize) + **〜たいですか** (want to do? — polite question form of desire).

**これは〜です** = 'this is ~': the basic [topic] は [predicate] です structure.
**これ** (kore) = 'this' (standalone pronoun, near the speaker). Before a noun use **この**: **この曲** (this song).
Japanese quotation marks **「」** are used for titles and direct quotes, equivalent to Western ''.

sentences.base_language: Aunti asked me if I have so many girlfriends
sentences.target_language: おばさんは私に彼女がたくさんいるか聞いた
sentences.romanized: obasan wa watashi ni kanojo ga takusan iru ka kiita
sentences.literal: Auntie [topic] me [direction] girlfriends [subj] many exist whether asked.
sentences.grammarNote:
**おばさん** (obasan) = aunt / middle-aged woman. Contrast with **おばあさん** (obaasan, grandmother/elderly woman) — the length of the vowel matters!
**〜いるか聞いた** = 'asked whether ~ exist': **いる** (iru, exist for living things) + **か** (embedded question marker) + **聞いた** (kiita, asked — plain past).
**私に聞く** (watashi ni kiku) = 'ask me': **に** marks the person asked.

sentences.base_language: Today I have one, tomorrow I'll have another, eh
sentences.target_language: 今日は一人、明日はまた別の人、えへ
sentences.romanized: kyou wa hitori, ashita wa mata betsu no hito, ehe
sentences.literal: Today [topic] one-person, tomorrow [topic] again different person, hehe.
sentences.grammarNote:
**一人** (hitori) = one person (irregular native counter). **また** (mata) = again/once more.
**別の** (betsu no) = 'different / another': **別** (betsu, separate/different) + **の** (connecting to following noun). **別の人** = 'a different person'.
The topic marker **は** after time words sets up a contrast: **今日は** vs **明日は** (today vs tomorrow) — a natural Japanese rhetorical parallel.

sentences.base_language: hey, but there's no wedding, there's no wedding, auntie
sentences.target_language: ねえ、でも結婚式はないよ、結婚式はないよ、おばさん
sentences.romanized: nee, demo kekkonshiki wa nai yo, kekkonshiki wa nai yo, obasan
sentences.literal: Hey, but wedding [topic] not-exist [emphasis], wedding not-exist [emphasis], auntie.
sentences.grammarNote: よ (yo) at the end of a sentence is a sentence-final particle that asserts or emphasizes information, like saying 'you know!' or adding conviction to a statement.

sentences.base_language: I'm going to take them all to the V.I.P., the V.I.P., hey
sentences.target_language: みんなをVIPルームに連れて行くよ、VIPルームに、ねえ
sentences.romanized: minna wo VIP ruumu ni tsurete iku yo, VIP ruumu ni, nee
sentences.literal: Everyone [obj] VIP room [direction] bring-go [emphasis], VIP room [direction], hey.
sentences.grammarNote:
**連れて行く** (tsurete iku) is a compound: **連れる** (to bring/accompany) + **行く** (to go). The te-form connects sequential or accompanying actions.
**みんな** (minna) = everyone/all of them: takes **を** as the object of **連れて行く**.
**ねえ** (nee) = a casual attention-getting particle or soft exclamation, like 'hey' or 'you know?'. Softer and more conversational than **ね**.

**挨拶** (aisatsu) = greeting: the kanji **挨** and **拶** both relate to approaching or pressing forward — together they mean 'exchange of greetings'.
**〜に挨拶する** = 'greet [someone]': **に** marks the person greeted.
**して** is the te-form used as a soft imperative: **挨拶して** = 'go ahead and greet [her]' — less forceful than **してください** but still a directive.

sentences.base_language: Let's take a selfie, "say cheese," hey
sentences.target_language: 自撮りしよう、「はい、チーズ」、ねえ
sentences.grammarNote:
**自撮り** (jidori) = selfie: **自** (ji, self) + **撮り** (tori, from **撮る**, to photograph). A modern compound word.
**〜しよう** = 'let's do ~': the volitional form of **する** (to do). **自撮りしよう** = 'let's take a selfie'.
**はい** (hai) = 'yes / okay / say...' — in this context it's a countdown prompt, like 'say cheese'. **チーズ** (chīzu) = cheese.

sentences.base_language: Let the ones I already slept with smile
sentences.target_language: もう寝た子たちは笑って
sentences.romanized: mou neta ko tachi wa waratte
sentences.grammarNote:
**もう寝た** = 'already slept': **もう** (mō, already) + **寝た** (neta, plain past of **寝る**, to sleep).
**子たち** (kotachi) = the [kids/ones]: **子** (ko, child/one) + **たち** (pluralizing suffix for people).
**笑って** = te-form used as a mild command: 'smile / go ahead and smile'. Less direct than **笑ってください**.

sentences.base_language: In a V.I.P., a V.I.P., hey
sentences.target_language: VIPルームで、VIPルームで、ねえ
sentences.romanized: VIP ruumu de, VIP ruumu de, nee
sentences.grammarNote:
**VIPルーム** = VIP room: **ルーム** (rūmu) is a loanword for room. Katakana English loanwords are very common in modern Japanese.
**で** marks the location where something happens: **VIPルームで** = 'in the VIP room'. Contrast: **に** (where something exists or goes to).
The repetition **VIPルームで、VIPルームで** mirrors the original Spanish lyric's rhythm — a borrowed pop culture phrase.

**挨拶** (aisatsu) = greeting/salutation. Repeated from the earlier line in the song.
**おばさん** (obasan) = aunt or middle-aged woman. Note the vowel length: **おばさん** (aunt/ma'am) vs **おばあさん** (grandmother) — a very important distinction.
The te-form **して** as a soft command repeats the call-and-response structure of the song.

sentences.target_language: 自撮りしよう、「はい、チーズ」
sentences.grammarNote:
**自撮りしよう** = 'let's take a selfie': volitional **〜しよう** (let's do). The volitional form of **する** is irregular: **する** → **しよう**.
**「はい、チーズ」** mirrors the English 'say cheese' — **はい** (hai) here is a prompt/countdown, and **チーズ** is directly borrowed as the smile-inducing word.
The repetition of this line without **ねえ** at the end reflects a slight variation in the lyrical structure.
